The wind shifted direction twice in one afternoon. The older folk say that means something.
Rain came down so hard the drainage channels overflowed and flooded the lower storage.
It was not the cold that got to people. It was the damp. Everything felt wet for days even after the skies cleared.
The fog stayed low and did not burn off until well past midday. You could not see ten paces ahead on the road.
Lightning struck the watchtower and split the beam clean through. Nobody was up there, thankfully.
We had three clear days in a row, which was enough for the builders to finish the outer wall. Then the rain came back like it was making up for lost time.
The hail was the size of river stones. Put holes in every canvas awning in the market square.
A dust storm blew through the pass and coated everything in a fine grit. Took a week to clean the well filters.
The temperature dropped so fast the puddles froze while people were still outside working.
There was a stillness before it hit. No breeze, no birdsong. Then the sky turned the color of a bruise and it all came down at once.
The dry spell lasted long enough that the reservoir dropped below the intake line. Rationing started on the third week.
A warm front rolled in from the south and melted the frost overnight. Turned every slope into a mudslide.
The clouds sat on the ridge for five days straight. No rain, just gray. People started getting short-tempered from the gloom.
We lost two rooftops to the gust that came through just before dawn. Nobody heard it coming because the wind was already loud.
The weather has been wrong all season. Warm when it should be cold, dry when it should be wet. The farmers do not know what to plant.
There was a ring around the moon three nights running. The sentries say that means heavy weather within the week.
The storm passed to the north of us but we could see it the whole time. Walls of rain moving across the valley like a curtain being drawn.
The barometric pressure dropped so sharply that the sealed jars in the storehouse popped their lids. The preserves are ruined and the quartermaster is furious.
It rained for eleven straight days. By the end of it, the mortar between the foundation stones had started to soften. The masons say we caught it just in time.
A funnel cloud touched down across the river. Did not reach us, but you could hear the sound from here: like a loaded cart being dragged over gravel.
The frost came three weeks earlier than the almanac predicted. Half the late harvest was lost because nobody had time to pull it in.
There was a break in the clouds just wide enough for the sun to come through. It lit up the rain like a wall of gold. Beautiful, if you had time to look.
The wind knocked the bell loose from the watchtower frame. It rolled halfway down the hill before anyone caught it.
The ground was so dry it cracked in patterns. Fissures wide enough to lose a boot heel in.
A sleet storm came through at an angle and coated the western face of every building. Ice a finger thick on every wall.
We measured the rainfall with a graduated jar and it exceeded the highest mark in a single afternoon. The record had stood for six years.
The sunrise was blood red for four mornings running. The fishermen refused to go out. I thought it was superstition, but the storm that followed was the worst in memory.
A whiteout rolled in while the patrol was still on the ridge. They had to rope themselves together to make it back. Lost a pack mule.
The humidity was so intense that metal tools started rusting overnight. The smith had to oil every piece in the workshop twice a day.
The snow was wet and heavy, the kind that collapses rooftops. We spent half the night shoveling the flat roofs clear.
A clear sky at midday turned black within an hour. The council had just started an outdoor assembly and everyone scattered.
The creek froze solid for the first time in living memory. Children were walking on it before anyone thought to check the thickness.
There was an electrical charge in the air before the storm. You could feel it on your skin, and sparks jumped between iron fittings.
The season changed overnight. One day it was warm enough to work without a coat, the next the water troughs had ice on them.
Wind stripped the thatch off three rooftops on the south row. The council is now requiring heavier weighting stones, which I think is long overdue.
A fog bank settled so thick that the sentries could not see the gate from the tower. They had to post someone at ground level, which defeats the purpose of having a tower.
The rain was warm, which sounds pleasant until you realize it means the water table is shifting. Warm rain in that season is not normal.
The last thunderstorm cracked a boulder on the eastern slope clean in half. Lightning, most likely, though nobody saw the strike itself.
We had a stretch of perfect weather: mild, dry, light breeze from the west. Everyone said it was too good to last. They were right.
Hail punched through the greenhouse panels. Three of the four frames will need to be rebuilt entirely. The seedlings are gone.
An ice storm left the road glazed over for two days. Even the draft animals could not keep their footing.
The dew point was so high that everything dripped. You could wring moisture out of a dry-hung cloak by midmorning.
A downdraft hit the market square and scattered a vendor's entire display. Papers, fabrics, the lot. Nobody helped pick them up, which tells you something about that vendor.
The heat index was dangerous for three consecutive days. The council ordered all heavy labor stopped, which was the right call even though the builders complained.
A rainbow appeared on the eastern horizon after the storm. Double arc, bright enough to cast visible color on the ground. The children thought it was magic.
We logged the wind speed by how far it carried the debris. A roofing tile landed two hundred paces from the nearest building.
The clouds had a greenish cast before the hail started. That color is unmistakable once you have seen it. I cleared the livestock in time but others did not.
A sandstorm stripped the paint off the southern palisade in a single afternoon. Three coats, gone. The woodworker was more impressed than angry.
The morning dew froze into crystals so thick the grass looked like it was made of glass. Stunning to look at, treacherous to walk on.
The barometer in the watch house has been dropping steadily for two days. The watch captain is pretending not to worry, but he moved the supply crates indoors.
There was a microburst that flattened a stand of young trees near the east gate. Perfect circle of destruction, maybe forty paces across, and the surrounding forest untouched.
The overnight temperature swing was forty degrees. Warm enough to sweat at sundown, cold enough to see your breath at dawn. Hard on the body and harder on the masonry.
A waterspout formed over the lake and stayed visible for almost ten minutes. Most of us had never seen one before. The fishermen have, and they were not impressed.
The overcast lasted so long that someone started tallying the days on the doorframe of the tavern. Seventeen marks before the sun came back.
A dry lightning storm hit the ridge with no rain to follow. Three brush fires started before the first patrol could reach them.
The snow came down in clumps the size of a fist. Not flakes: wet, heavy masses. You could hear them hitting the roof like thrown stones.
The air pressure changed so abruptly that the doors in the main hall all slammed shut at once. Startled everyone in the building.
There was a warm wind from the south that lasted exactly one night. By morning the air was cold again, but the snow had melted off every south-facing surface.
The thunderclaps were so close together it sounded like a continuous roar. I have never seen the dogs react that badly.
A sudden cold snap froze the laundry on the lines stiff as boards. Someone broke a shirt trying to fold it, which would be funny if we had shirts to spare.
The rain fell in sheets so dense that you could see individual curtains of water moving across the fields. Like waves, but vertical.
The wind chill made it feel thirty degrees colder than the actual temperature. The sentries were rotating every half hour instead of every two hours.
An early thaw weakened the ice on the upper pond and a supply cart broke through. We recovered the goods but the cart is still down there.
The sky had three distinct layers of cloud moving in different directions. The weather-reader said it meant conflicting fronts, and she advised against traveling. She was right.
Condensation formed inside the storehouses so heavily that the ceiling was dripping. Everything underneath had to be covered or moved.
The heat cracked the clay tiles on the eastern roof. Expansion, the mason says. I say whoever fired those tiles used the wrong mix.
A line of storms came through in waves: hit, clear, hit, clear. Four cycles in one night. Nobody slept.
The morning fog smelled of sulfur. Nobody could explain it. The well water tasted fine, so it was not the ground. My guess is volcanic, though the nearest vent is weeks away.
The sun was so bright after three weeks of overcast that people were shielding their eyes just to cross the square. We had all adjusted to the gray.
An inversion layer trapped the smoke from every hearth inside the settlement walls. By noon the air was thick enough to sting the eyes.
There was ball lightning rolling along the ridge during the last storm. Three sentries saw it independently, and their descriptions match perfectly.
The cloud ceiling dropped so low you could almost touch it from the watchtower platform. Disorienting to look up and see nothing but flat gray.
A squall hit the harbor with no warning. Two boats were swamped before the crews could reef the sails. The harbormaster is blaming the weather-reader, which I think is unfair.
The relative humidity has been above ninety for a week. Bread molds within a day of baking, leather swells, and everyone's joints ache.
A derecho-class wind event flattened everything south of the main road. Tents, awnings, fencing: all flat. Permanent structures held but windows did not.
The freezing rain made every surface a mirror. Two people broke bones just walking to the well. The council finally ordered sand spread on the paths, three hours too late.
The aurora was visible for the first time in years. Green and violet curtains across the northern sky. The elders say it is an omen. The astronomers say it is particles.
The drought killed the lower well entirely. Went from producing to dry in under a week. The deep aquifer must have dropped below the bore depth.
A persistent crosswind made archery practice impossible. The arrows were drifting a full body-width at fifty paces.
The dew was so heavy it looked like it had rained. Every surface was soaked by dawn. Good for the gardens, bad for anyone who left leather goods outside.
The sky cleared just before sunset and the temperature plummeted. We lost five saplings in the nursery because nobody thought to cover them.
A gust of wind caught the main gate and slammed it hard enough to crack the upper hinge. The blacksmith says it is the third time this season.
The lightning was striking the same ridge over and over, maybe twenty times in an hour. The rockfall the next morning was no surprise.
There is a pattern the old-timers track: three mild winters, then a brutal one. We are due for the brutal one and the wood stores are not ready.
The rain gutters on the main hall are undersized for this kind of volume. Every heavy rain turns the front entrance into a waterfall. The council knows and does nothing.
A chinook blew in and raised the temperature by twenty degrees in less than an hour. The snowpack on the western slope turned to slush and two trails washed out.
The sky turned yellow before the dust front hit. Like looking through stained glass. Then visibility dropped to nothing for almost an hour.

A microclimate near the hot springs keeps that patch of ground warm even when everything else is frozen. The herbalist plants there year-round and others think she is lucky. She is not lucky. She is observant.
The last nor'easter drove rain horizontally through every gap in the shutters. The eastern rooms were ankle-deep by morning.
The clouds parted in a near-perfect circle directly overhead. We could see blue sky surrounded by solid gray in every direction. It closed up within an hour.
A persistent drizzle lasted nine days. Not enough rain to fill anything, but enough to keep everything wet. The worst kind of weather for morale.
The temperature inversion trapped cold air in the valley while the ridges were warm. People living on the heights were working in light clothes while we were bundled up twenty feet below.
The thunderhead was the tallest I have ever seen. The top was flat as an anvil and you could see the ice crystals catching the sunlight at the crown.
A flash freeze turned the road into an ice sheet in under an hour. A wagon lost traction on the grade and slid sideways into the ditch. Took six people to right it.
The wind was constant and unvarying for three days. Same speed, same direction, no gusts. It was more unsettling than a storm because storms at least change.
The first real snow of the season always catches someone off guard. This year it was the traders, who had three carts of unsheltered goods. Their loss, but our prices go up because of it.
A wall of fog rolled in from the lowlands and stopped at the gate as if it had hit a barrier. The air inside the walls was perfectly clear while outside was blind.
The weather-reader predicted clear skies and was wrong by twelve hours. The outdoor market was soaked. She has been right the last forty times, but nobody remembers those.
Heat lightning flashed along the horizon all night without a single drop of rain. The sky flickered like a distant forge. Kept the sentries on edge for nothing.
The frost patterns on the windows were unusually geometric this morning. Crystalline lattices, very precise. The glassmaker says it depends on the mineral content of the condensation.
We measured the snowfall at three hands deep. The council tried to organize a clearing crew but half the settlement just stayed indoors and waited for it to melt.
A southerly wind brought the smell of wildfire from at least two valleys over. No smoke visible, just the scent. Sharp enough to worry the livestock.
The rain was acidic enough to etch the limestone facade of the meeting hall. The masons identified it by the white streaking. They blame the quarry operations upstream.
A sustained downpour revealed every leak in every roof simultaneously. The coopers had a backlog of bucket orders for a week.
The barometric drop before the last storm was so steep that the pressure gauge in the watch house bottomed out. The watch captain has ordered a replacement with a wider range.

An unseasonable warm spell tricked the fruit trees into blooming early. When the frost returned, it killed every blossom. No fruit this year, and the orchardist blames the council for not funding windbreaks.
The cloud formations were stacked in layers moving in opposing directions. The weather-reader said it meant shear conditions and advised grounding the messenger birds. Wise call.
A freezing mist coated every branch, wire, and railing in a thick layer of rime. Beautiful to look at, but the weight brought down two old branches and a clothesline.
The storm surge raised the river four feet above its normal bank. The lower fields were under water for two days and the topsoil washed downstream.
The wind carried topsoil from the plowed fields and deposited it against every building on the east side. Drifts of dirt two feet high in some doorways.
An abrupt calm followed the storm. No wind, no sound, nothing. The silence was more unsettling than the thunder had been.
The overnight frost penetrated deeper than the root line of the winter crops. We will not know the full extent of the damage until spring.
A layer of graupel covered the ground before the snow started. Like walking on gravel made of ice. The sentries said their footsteps were audible from a hundred paces.
The sun dogs appeared on both sides of the sun at midday. Two false suns flanking the real one. The older sentries took it as a weather warning and doubled the watch.

The wind funneled through the gap between the main hall and the storehouse at twice the speed it hit the outer walls. A design flaw nobody considered until the sign over the tavern door was torn off its bracket.
The spring thaw came in stages: a day of melt, a night of refreeze, repeat. The result was a layer cake of ice and water that made the paths treacherous for nearly two weeks.
A warm rain fell during a cold snap and froze on contact with every surface. The entire settlement was glazed in ice by dawn. Even the door latches were frozen shut.
The storm knocked out the bridge pilings on the lower crossing. The council authorized emergency repairs, but the contractor says two weeks minimum. The detour adds half a day to every supply run.
The weather has been cycling between extremes with no transition. Hot to cold, wet to dry, calm to gale. The livestock are stressed and the farmers are worse.
There was a double rainbow visible for nearly an hour after the storm. The entire settlement stopped to look. Even the guard on duty turned around, which tells you how rare it was.
The ground fog at dawn was thick enough that only the rooftops were visible from the watchtower. The settlement looked like it was floating on a white sea.
A sudden hailstorm lasted exactly four minutes and then stopped as if someone had closed a door. The ground was white with ice and the sky was clear blue. No explanation.
The prevailing wind reversed direction for the first time in anyone's memory. The smoke from the forges, which always drifts east, was blowing west. The weather-reader had no precedent for it.
The rain was so consistent for so long that the millpond overflowed its spillway and flooded the lower workshop. The miller and the tanner have been arguing about drainage rights ever since.
The last real cold snap dropped the temperature below what the masonry can tolerate. We found cracks in the north wall where water had frozen inside the stone and expanded.
A sustained gale bent the flagpole on the watch house to a thirty-degree angle. The metalworker straightened it, but she says the iron has fatigued and it will snap next time.
The early morning dew combined with the overnight temperature drop to create a layer of black ice invisible to the eye. The watch captain slipped and bruised two ribs.

The sky turned copper-colored at sunset after the volcanic ash drifted in from the south. The particles stayed in the air for a week and the sunsets were spectacular, if you could ignore what it meant.
A squall line passed through in twenty minutes and dropped more water than the previous ten days combined. The drainage system backed up and the lower storehouse took two inches of standing water.

The wind howled through the arrow slits in the wall all night. A constant, high-pitched whistle that made sleep impossible. The masons are stuffing the gaps with oakum, which is a fix but not a solution.

There has been no measurable precipitation in six weeks. The council is debating rationing, the farmers are debating crop priorities, and nobody is debating the fact that they should have started both conversations three weeks ago.
The heat made the road surface soft enough to take footprints. By noon the main path looked like a tracking exercise.

A line squall hit the market during peak hours. Every stall awning went at once: a chain reaction of fabric and splintered poles. The merchants are demanding covered stalls and the council says there is no budget.

The overnight condensation on the metal roof of the armory was heavy enough to drip all morning. The watch captain moved the bowstrings to a dry room, which is the kind of thinking that justifies his position.
The windbreak trees on the western approach were planted by the second generation of settlers. Three of them came down in the last gale and nobody has planted replacements. Short-sighted, in my view.

A temperature gradient between the valley floor and the ridge creates a daily cycle of upslope and downslope winds. Predictable as clockwork if you bother to learn the timing. Most people do not bother.
The rain gauge overflowed, so we do not have an accurate measurement for the last storm. The weather-reader is annoyed. I told her to get a bigger gauge. She did not appreciate the suggestion.

A layer of sea salt deposited by the coastal wind corroded the iron fittings on the eastern gate. The blacksmith had to replace all twelve hinges. She is billing the council, and I doubt they will pay promptly.
The updrafts near the cliffs were strong enough to carry debris to the top of the watchtower. We found leaves and small branches on the platform, deposited there by wind alone.
The weather broke cleanly at the ridgeline. Standing on the crest, you could see rain to the east and clear sky to the west. A sharp division, no gradual transition.
There was a period of absolute calm between two storm bands. Maybe twenty minutes of silence, no wind, not even a breeze. Then it started again from the opposite direction.
The relative humidity dropped to near zero during the dry wind event. Skin cracked, lips split, and the fire risk went through the roof. The watch doubled patrols near the thatched buildings.
A sustained southeast wind pushed the lake water to one end and exposed the shallows at the other. Fish were stranded in pools. Free food if you were quick enough.
The weather-reader uses a combination of cloud reading, pressure changes, and joint pain to make her predictions. She is right more often than the almanac. I have learned not to question her methods.
The thermal shock from the rapid cooling cracked the glazing on the kiln. A full firing's worth of pottery ruined because the weather turned while the kiln was still open.

A persistent low-pressure system has been sitting over us for a week. Everyone is sluggish, headachy, and irritable. The healer says it is the pressure. The council says it is laziness. The healer is right.
The last snowfall was the heavy, wet kind that builds up on horizontal surfaces. The weight collapsed a section of the animal pen roof. No livestock injured, but only by luck.

The wind has been stripping shingles off the east-facing roofs all season. The supply of replacement shingles ran out two weeks ago and the next shipment is delayed. We are patching with whatever we can find.
A freak warm spell in midwinter melted enough snow to fill the creek past flood stage. The bridge held but the approaches washed out on both sides. Foot traffic only for the next week.
The static charge in the air before the storm was strong enough to make your hair stand. The smith was throwing sparks off the anvil without striking it.
The cloud base was so low that the ridge sentries were inside the clouds. They reported visibility of arm's length and the sound of thunder below them.

There was a window of fair weather that lasted exactly long enough for the supply convoy to get through. Three hours of clear sky between two storm fronts. The timing was either lucky or blessed, depending on who you ask.

The overnight low was cold enough to freeze the ink in the record-keeper's office. She lost a full page of entries when the bottle cracked. Now she keeps it by the fire, which is a risk of a different kind.

A virga formation was visible over the eastern ridge: rain falling from clouds but evaporating before it reached the ground. Teasing. The farmers watched it with the kind of resentment you usually reserve for people.
The permafrost line shifted noticeably uphill this season. Ground that used to stay frozen year-round is now soft. The foundations built on that ground are starting to settle unevenly.
